items.q: What is the difference between a phoneme and a grapheme?
items.correct: A phoneme is the smallest unit of sound in a language; a grapheme is the written letter or letters that represent it.
items.misconception: Phonemes are letters and graphemes are syllables.
items.random_wrong: Phonemes are vowels and graphemes are consonants.
items.why: Phonemes are sounds, graphemes are written symbols. The grapheme 'sh' represents one phoneme; the grapheme 'c' can represent two (/k/ or /s/). Students treat them as the same thing.

items.q: What is the subject of a sentence?
items.correct: The noun phrase that performs the action of the verb (in active voice) or that the verb is predicated about — not necessarily what the sentence is 'about'.
items.misconception: The subject is whatever the sentence is talking about.
items.random_wrong: The subject is always the first word in the sentence.
items.why: In 'The cake was eaten by John', the subject is 'the cake' (grammatically), though the sentence is 'about' John. Students conflate topic with grammatical subject.

items.q: How are word classes determined?
items.correct: By the word's grammatical function in a sentence — not its meaning. The same word can belong to different classes depending on use (e.g. 'run' as noun or verb).
items.misconception: A noun is always a person, place or thing.
items.random_wrong: A noun is any word that starts with a capital letter.
items.why: 'Running' can be a noun ('Running is healthy'), a verb ('I am running'), or an adjective ('running water'). The notional definition fails — function in context determines word class.

items.q: What did Chomsky propose about language acquisition?
items.correct: Children possess an innate Language Acquisition Device — an inborn capacity for grammar — because exposure alone cannot account for how rapidly and uniformly children learn complex syntax.
items.misconception: Chomsky said children learn language entirely by copying adults.
items.random_wrong: Chomsky said children should be taught grammar from age two.
items.why: Chomsky's theory is precisely the opposite of behaviourist imitation. Students confuse Chomsky with Skinner — Chomsky's argument is that imitation cannot explain acquisition.

items.q: What is Skinner's behaviourist view of language acquisition?
items.correct: Children learn language through imitation and operant conditioning — they are reinforced for correct utterances and corrected for errors.
items.misconception: Skinner believed in an innate grammar module.
items.random_wrong: Skinner believed children couldn't learn language until age five.
items.why: Skinner is the BEHAVIOURIST — the opposite of nativists like Chomsky. Students mix up the two. Skinner's view is environmental; Chomsky's is innate.

items.q: What is Vygotsky's view of language development?
items.correct: Language develops through social interaction with more knowledgeable others — they 'scaffold' the child within the Zone of Proximal Development.
items.misconception: Vygotsky believed children develop language alone through internal processes.
items.random_wrong: Vygotsky was a behaviourist who emphasised reinforcement.
items.why: Vygotsky's central insight is the SOCIAL nature of cognition and language — opposite of internal-only theories like Piaget's. Students confuse him with individualist theorists.

items.q: What is register in language?
items.correct: A variety of language used in a particular social setting — varying by formality, field (topic) and mode (spoken/written) to suit audience and purpose.
items.misconception: Register is just how loud you speak.
items.random_wrong: Register is the same as accent.
items.why: Students confuse register with volume or accent. Register concerns appropriateness to context — formal vs informal vocabulary, sentence structure and politeness markers.

items.q: What is the difference between a dialect and an accent?
items.correct: Accent refers only to pronunciation; dialect includes vocabulary and grammar as well as pronunciation — so all dialects involve accents but not vice versa.
items.misconception: Dialect and accent mean the same thing.
items.random_wrong: A dialect is a foreign language; an accent is how locals speak.
items.why: The two terms are commonly conflated in everyday use. In linguistics, accent is a subset of dialect — dialect is broader, covering all variation, accent only sound.

items.q: What is the difference between descriptive and prescriptive approaches to language?
items.correct: Descriptivists describe how language is actually used; prescriptivists rule on how language ought to be used — modern linguistics is descriptivist.
items.misconception: Descriptivists describe written language and prescriptivists describe spoken language.
items.random_wrong: Both terms mean the same — analysing language scientifically.
items.why: The split is about attitude, not mode. Descriptivists treat all varieties as valid; prescriptivists make value judgements. Students mistake it for a written/spoken distinction.

items.q: What is a morpheme?
items.correct: The smallest unit of meaning in a language — a word like 'unhappiness' has three morphemes: un-, happy, and -ness.
items.misconception: A morpheme is a syllable.
items.random_wrong: A morpheme is a sound that has no meaning.
items.why: Morphemes are units of meaning; syllables are units of sound. 'Strength' is one syllable but two morphemes (strong + th). Students conflate the two units.

items.q: What is the difference between semantics and pragmatics?
items.correct: Semantics studies literal meaning of words and sentences; pragmatics studies how meaning depends on context, speaker intention and conversational implicature.
items.misconception: Semantics is grammar and pragmatics is vocabulary.
items.random_wrong: Semantics is speech and pragmatics is writing.
items.why: 'Can you pass the salt?' semantically asks ability; pragmatically requests salt. Students miss that pragmatics governs the gap between literal meaning and intended meaning.

items.q: Why do text types have predictable features?
items.correct: Conventions develop because they serve communicative purpose — a recipe needs imperatives because it instructs; a news report needs the inverted pyramid because of how readers scan.
items.misconception: Text types have fixed features just because tradition says so.
items.random_wrong: Text types are arbitrary and have no patterns.
items.why: Conventions are functional, not arbitrary. Students treat genre features as rules to memorise; understanding their purpose explains why they exist and when they can be broken.

items.q: What does Labov's research show about language variation?
items.correct: Language varies systematically by social class, gender, age and context — and speakers shift style depending on formality, often unconsciously.
items.misconception: Labov showed that working-class people speak ungrammatically.
items.random_wrong: Labov showed that everyone speaks the same way regardless of background.
items.why: Labov's finding is that variation is rule-governed, not deficient. Working-class varieties follow consistent grammars — they aren't broken Standard English. Students misread variation as error.

items.q: Why does English vocabulary have so many synonyms?
items.correct: Layered historical contact — Old English, Old Norse, Norman French and Latin — left English with multiple words from different sources for similar concepts (e.g. ask/question/interrogate).
items.misconception: English has many synonyms because Shakespeare invented lots of words.
items.random_wrong: Because English speakers have always wanted to make things complicated.
items.why: Shakespeare invented many words, but the underlying layered lexis comes from successive waves of invasion and contact — Anglo-Saxon, Norse, French, Latin. Each layer added vocabulary.

items.q: What is Grice's cooperative principle?
items.correct: The assumption that speakers cooperate in conversation by following maxims of quantity, quality, relation and manner — flouting these creates implicature.
items.misconception: The cooperative principle means people are always nice to each other.
items.random_wrong: The cooperative principle means everyone must agree in conversation.
items.why: Cooperation is technical — about the structure of communication, not politeness. Speakers can disagree fiercely while still cooperating in Grice's sense. Students collapse it into social niceness.

items.q: What is Received Pronunciation (RP)?
items.correct: A prestige accent associated historically with public schools and the BBC — spoken by a small minority of Britons; it is an accent, not a dialect.
items.misconception: RP is the correct way to speak English.
items.random_wrong: RP is the accent everyone in southern England uses.
items.why: RP has high social prestige but no inherent correctness. Descriptive linguistics treats it as one accent among many — students absorb the prescriptive notion of 'correct' speech.

items.q: What is the telegraphic stage of child language development?
items.correct: Around age 2-3, children produce short utterances containing content words but omitting function words and inflections (e.g. 'mummy gone shop').
items.misconception: It is the stage when children first start using a telephone.
items.random_wrong: It is the stage where children memorise long stories.
items.why: The name comes from telegrams — which dropped articles and prepositions to save money. Students misread the term literally rather than understanding the analogy.

items.q: Are features of spoken language like 'um' and 'you know' signs of poor speech?
items.correct: No — they are functional discourse markers and planning devices that help interaction flow, manage turn-taking and signal speaker meaning.
items.misconception: Fillers and false starts show the speaker is uneducated.
items.random_wrong: Spoken language has no rules unlike written language.
items.why: Spoken features serve real purposes (hedging, turn-holding, processing time). Students apply written-language standards to speech, treating its natural features as failure rather than as features of a different mode.

items.q: Why might a writer choose the passive voice?
items.correct: To foreground the patient rather than the agent, omit the agent when unknown or unimportant, sound formal/scientific, or distance the writer from action.
items.misconception: The passive voice is always grammatically wrong.
items.random_wrong: Passive voice means writing in the past tense.
items.why: Passive is grammatically fine and often appropriate. Prescriptive advice against it is overstated. Students absorb 'never use passive' rules without understanding the meaningful choices it enables.

items.q: What is Standard English?
items.correct: A dialect that has gained social and institutional prestige — used in education and formal writing; it is one variety, not inherently superior in linguistic terms.
items.misconception: Standard English is the original and correct form of the language.
items.random_wrong: Standard English is the same as Received Pronunciation.
items.why: Standard English is one historical dialect (originally East Midlands) that gained prestige. It is not older or more logical than other varieties. Students confuse standard with RP (Standard is grammar/lexis; RP is accent).

items.q: What is a semantic field?
items.correct: A group of words from a similar area of meaning — e.g. 'whisper, scream, mumble, shout' all belong to the semantic field of vocalisation.
items.misconception: A semantic field is a list of synonyms for one word.
items.random_wrong: A semantic field is a field of study like physics or biology.
items.why: Synonyms share meaning closely; semantic fields are broader, including related but distinct words. 'Hot' and 'cold' belong to the same semantic field (temperature) but aren't synonyms.

items.q: What is convergence in accommodation theory?
items.correct: When a speaker adjusts their speech to be more similar to their interlocutor's — to express solidarity, gain approval or ease communication.
items.misconception: Convergence is when two speakers start using identical accents permanently.
items.random_wrong: Convergence means two languages merge into one new language.
items.why: Convergence is a temporary, contextual style shift — not a permanent change. Students confuse it with language change at a community level rather than individual interactional choice.

items.q: What is overextension in child language?
items.correct: When a child applies a word more broadly than adults do — e.g. calling all four-legged animals 'dog' before learning distinct categories.
items.misconception: Overextension is when children use very long words they don't understand.
items.random_wrong: Overextension is when children speak too loudly.
items.why: Overextension is about semantic categories, not word length or volume. It shows children are forming hypotheses about meaning, then refining them — a key window into acquisition.

items.q: Is language change a sign of decline?
items.correct: No — modern linguistics treats change as natural and continuous, not as decay. Every language has always been changing and will continue to.
items.misconception: Language change shows that standards are falling and English is being ruined.
items.random_wrong: Language change only happens in informal speech, not in writing.
items.why: The 'decline' view is prescriptivist folk-belief. Linguists observe that what one generation laments as 'wrong' is often what previous generations also lamented and became standard — change is constant.

items.q: Are claims that women use more 'tentative' language well-supported?
items.correct: Early studies (Lakoff) made broad claims, but later research (e.g. Holmes, Cameron) shows context matters more than gender, and many supposed differences disappear with proper data.
items.misconception: Women always use more hedges and tag questions than men.
items.random_wrong: Women and men have different vocal cords so speak differently.
items.why: Stereotypes about gendered language are widely repeated in textbooks but contested by current research. Students inherit Lakoff's claims without the critique that has followed.

items.q: What is the IPA in linguistics?
items.correct: The International Phonetic Alphabet — a standardised set of symbols where each symbol represents one distinct sound. Used to transcribe pronunciation precisely across all languages.
items.misconception: The IPA is just another alphabet like the Roman or Greek alphabet.
items.random_wrong: The IPA stands for International Phonetic Acronym.
items.why: Ordinary alphabets have ambiguous symbol-sound mapping (English 'ough' has multiple pronunciations). The IPA exists precisely because regular alphabets are insufficient — one symbol = one sound, universally.

items.q: What is the difference between voiced and voiceless sounds?
items.correct: Voiced sounds are produced with vocal-fold vibration (/b/, /d/, /g/, /z/); voiceless sounds without it (/p/, /t/, /k/, /s/). You can feel the difference by putting a hand on your throat.
items.misconception: Voiced sounds are loud and voiceless sounds are whispered.
items.random_wrong: Voiced sounds use the lips; voiceless sounds use the tongue.
items.why: Voicing is about vocal-fold vibration, not volume. Whisper has no voicing at all but isn't silent. Students confuse phonation with loudness, missing how voicing distinguishes minimal pairs (pin/bin).

items.q: What is the difference between free and bound morphemes?
items.correct: Free morphemes can stand alone as words ('cat', 'happy', 'run'); bound morphemes must attach to another morpheme (prefixes, suffixes — 'un-', '-ness', '-ing').
items.misconception: Free morphemes are free to use and bound morphemes cost money to use.
items.random_wrong: Free morphemes are nouns and bound morphemes are verbs.
items.why: 'Free' and 'bound' refer to structural independence, not cost or word class. Students take 'free' literally or assign meaning by part of speech, missing the structural criterion.

items.q: What is the difference between inflectional and derivational morphology?
items.correct: Inflectional morphemes mark grammatical features without changing word class (cat/cats, walk/walked); derivational morphemes create new words, often changing word class (happy → happiness, teach → teacher).
items.misconception: Inflection and derivation are two words for the same process.
items.random_wrong: Inflectional morphology applies only to verbs and derivational only to nouns.
items.why: The difference matters for word formation rules. English has very limited inflection but rich derivation. Students conflate the two, missing how morphology builds vocabulary vs marks grammar.

items.q: What is the difference between transitive and intransitive verbs?
items.correct: Transitive verbs take a direct object ('she ate the cake'); intransitive verbs don't ('she slept'). Some verbs are both depending on use ('she ran' / 'she ran the company').
items.misconception: Transitive verbs are action verbs and intransitive are linking verbs.
items.random_wrong: Transitive verbs end in -ed and intransitive in -ing.
items.why: Transitivity is about object-taking, not action vs state. Both transitive and intransitive verbs can be action verbs. Students conflate transitivity with verb category, missing the syntactic criterion.

items.q: What is a noun phrase (NP)?
items.correct: A syntactic unit headed by a noun, which may include determiners, adjectives and modifiers — e.g. 'the big red car' is a noun phrase functioning as a single grammatical unit.
items.misconception: A noun phrase is just a noun by itself.
items.random_wrong: A noun phrase is a sentence that contains nouns.
items.why: Noun phrases can be one word ('cats') or many ('the cats that lived next door'). The phrase functions as a unit — subject, object or complement. Students reduce NP to a single noun, missing constituent structure.

items.q: What is code-switching?
items.correct: Alternating between languages or varieties within a conversation or even a single utterance — often patterned by context, topic, identity or audience. Code-switching is rule-governed, not random.
items.misconception: Code-switching shows the speaker doesn't know either language properly.
items.random_wrong: Code-switching means using slang words in formal speech.
items.why: Code-switching requires fluency in both codes and follows linguistic constraints (e.g. avoiding ungrammatical splits). Students treat it as evidence of deficiency — research shows it is a sophisticated bilingual skill.

items.q: What is the difference between overt and covert prestige?
items.correct: Overt prestige is the high status of standard varieties recognised by society (RP, Standard English); covert prestige is the hidden status of non-standard varieties within particular groups, signalling solidarity or toughness.
items.misconception: Only standard language has prestige; non-standard varieties have none.
items.random_wrong: Overt prestige is loud speech and covert prestige is quiet speech.
items.why: Covert prestige explains why people maintain non-standard varieties despite social stigma — they carry value within their communities. Students assume prestige is single-dimensional, missing why language change isn't all toward the standard.

items.q: How does Piaget's theory relate language to cognitive development?
items.correct: Language develops as a consequence of cognitive development — children must first develop concepts (object permanence, conservation) before they can express them linguistically. Cognition leads language.
items.misconception: Piaget said language drives cognitive development.
items.random_wrong: Piaget was a behaviourist who emphasised reinforcement.
items.why: Piaget's order is cognition first, then language — opposite of Whorfian linguistic determinism. Vygotsky disagreed, arguing language and thought interact. Students confuse the direction of Piaget's claim.

items.q: How does Bruner's view of language acquisition differ from Vygotsky's?
items.correct: Both emphasise social interaction, but Bruner proposes a Language Acquisition Support System (LASS) — adults provide structured, predictable interaction (book-reading, peekaboo) that 'scaffolds' acquisition. Vygotsky's scaffolding is broader (within ZPD across all learning).
items.misconception: Bruner and Vygotsky are saying the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: Bruner was a nativist who opposed all social theories.
items.why: LASS is a specific mechanism (interactive routines) Bruner proposed as the partner to Chomsky's LAD. Students conflate Bruner with Vygotsky, missing the distinctive interactional-routine concept.

items.q: What is a speech act according to Searle/Austin?
items.correct: An utterance that performs an action — promising, ordering, apologising, declaring. Speech acts have locutionary (what's said), illocutionary (intended force) and perlocutionary (effect) dimensions.
items.misconception: A speech act is any time someone speaks.
items.random_wrong: A speech act is a performance or a play.
items.why: Speech act theory analyses how language DOES things, not just describes things. 'I now pronounce you married' is a declaration, not a description. Students miss that utterances can be acts in themselves.

items.q: What is an adjacency pair in conversation analysis?
items.correct: A two-part sequence where the first part conditions the second — question/answer, greeting/greeting, request/acceptance-or-refusal. The second part is 'conditionally relevant' on the first.
items.misconception: Adjacency pairs are two people who sit next to each other while talking.
items.random_wrong: Adjacency pairs are pairs of synonyms in vocabulary lists.
items.why: Adjacency pairs reveal that turns aren't independent — a question expects an answer, and silence after one is heard as meaningful. Students take 'adjacency' too literally (physical proximity) rather than structural sequencing.

items.q: What is conversational implicature?
items.correct: Meaning conveyed beyond what is literally said — generated when speakers obey or deliberately flout Grice's maxims. 'Some students passed' implicates 'not all', via the maxim of quantity.
items.misconception: Implicature is the same as the literal meaning of a sentence.
items.random_wrong: Implicature is a kind of grammatical mistake.
items.why: Implicature is precisely the gap between said and meant. Students conflate literal and intended meaning, missing why pragmatics is needed at all — much of communication is implied, not stated.
